Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 59 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the yellow block did nothing (board_changed: False). So the yellow block is not interactive.
Let me look at the adjacency list more carefully. The adjacency list shows:
- [0, 3]: node 0 is adjacent to node 3
- [7, 9], [7, 10], [7, 11], [7, 12]: node 7 is adjacent to nodes 9, 10, 11, 12
Node 7 is likely the blue object (since it's adjacent to all 4 magenta blocks 9, 10, 11, 12). Wait, but the magenta blocks are at:
- Block 1: (36-37, 11-12)
- Block 2: (35-36, 32-33)
- Block 3: (51-52, 42-43)
- Block 4: (52-53, 11-12)
These are far from the blue object at (23-31, 29-37). So they can't be adjacent to the blue object.
Wait, let me re-check. The adjacency list says node 7 is adjacent to nodes 9, 10, 11, 12. But the magenta block ids are [3, 9, 10, 11, 12]. So there are 5 magenta blocks: ids 3, 9, 10, 11, 12.
Node 3 is adjacent to node 0. Nodes 9, 10, 11, 12 are adjacent to node 7.
Let me check what node 0 and node 7 are. Node 0 might be the white bar at the bottom, and node 7 might be the blue object.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the nodes are ordered top-most-left-most. So node 0 is likely the top-left colored block (S at 1-2, 1-2). Node 3 might be one of the magenta blocks.
Wait, the magenta block ids are [3, 9, 10, 11, 12]. Let me check the boundaries:
- Magenta blocks: boundaries [[1, 5], [1, 6], [2, 6], [2, 5]] (this is the M block in the top bar), [[35, 32], ...], [[36, 11], ...], [[51, 42], ...], [[52, 11], ...]
So the magenta blocks are:
- Id 3: (1-2, 5-6) - the M block in the top bar
- Id 9: (35-36, 32-33) - block 2
- Id 10: (36-37, 11-12) - block 1
- Id 11: (51-52, 42-43) - block 3
- Id 12: (52-53, 11-12) - block 4
Node 3 (M block in top bar) is adjacent to node 0 (S block in top bar). This makes sense since they're next to each other in the top bar.
Nodes 9, 10, 11, 12 (the 4 magenta blocks) are adjacent to node 7. Node 7 must be the blue object.
Wait, but the magenta blocks are far from the blue object. Let me re-check the positions:
- Block 2 (id 9): (35-36, 32-33)
- Block 1 (id 10): (36-37, 11-12)
- Block 3 (id 11): (51-52, 42-43)
- Block 4 (id 12): (52-53, 11-12)
The blue object is at (23-31, 29-37). Block 2 at (35-36, 32-33) is below the blue object (rows 35-36 vs 23-31). The column 32-33 overlaps with the blue object's column 29-37. But the rows don't overlap, so they're not adjacent.
Hmm, maybe node 7 is not the blue object. Let me check what node 7 is.
Actually, I think the adjacency list might be wrong or I'm misinterpreting it. Let me look at the segmentation more carefully.
Actually, wait. The adjacency list says nodes 9, 10, 11, 12 are all adjacent to node 7. But these nodes are the 4 magenta blocks, which are far apart. They can't all be adjacent to the same node unless node 7 is a large object that touches all of them.
The only large object that could touch all 4 magenta blocks is the black background. So node 7 might be the black background, and the adjacency list is showing that the magenta blocks are adjacent to the background.
OK, so the adjacency list doesn't give me useful information about the blue object.
Let me think about this differently. I've been moving the magenta blocks up-left, but the level is not completing. Maybe the goal is not to move the blocks up-left. Maybe the goal is to move them in a different direction or to specific positions.
But the only action I know is clicking a magenta block to move it up-left. There's no other action I know for moving the blocks.
Wait, maybe I need to click the pair positions again to teleport the blocks again? Let me try clicking one of the pair positions to see what happens.
The pair positions are:
- Pair 1: (41, 16) - but block 1 is now at (36-37, 11-12), far from (41, 16)
- Pair 2: (40, 37) - but block 2 is now at (35-36, 32-33), far from (40, 37)
- Pair 3: (56, 47) - but block 3 is now at (51-52, 42-43), far from (56, 47)
- Pair 4: (57, 16) - but block 4 is now at (52-53, 11-12), far from (57, 16)
The pair positions are no longer near the blocks. Clicking them might not do anything.
Actually, wait. In the previous attempt, I noticed that after teleporting a block, the pair position becomes part of the block (sticky). So clicking the pair position again doesn't move the block. But after moving the block up-left, the pair position is no longer part of the block. So clicking the pair position again might teleport the block back to the pair position.
Let me try clicking one of the pair positions to see what happens.
Actually, I realize I need to step back and think about the overall goal. The level has:
1. 4 pairs of sky blue dots
2. A blue object in the center
3. 4 colored blocks in the top-left (S, M, p, Y)
4. A yellow block at (3-6, 30-33)
The mechanic is:
- Click a sky blue dot to create a magenta 2x2 block
- Click a magenta block to move it up-left by (1, 1)
- Click the pair position to teleport the magenta block
I've created all 4 blocks, teleported them, and moved them up-left 4 times each. The level is still not complete.
Maybe the goal is to move the magenta blocks until they overlap with the blue object. But I've been moving them up-left, and they're still far from the blue object.
Wait, let me look at the blue object's position again. The blue object is at (23-31, 29-37). Block 2 is at (35-36, 32-33). If I move block 2 up-left, it moves to (34-35, 31-32), (33-34, 30-31), (32-33, 29-30), (31-32, 28-29), ...
At (31-32, 28-29), block 2 would overlap with the blue object (which is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37). The block's top-right corner (31, 29) would be at the blue object's bottom-left corner (31, 29). So they would touch at exactly one cell.
Let me try moving block 2 towards the blue object and see what happens. But I already tried this in the previous attempt, and the game ended. Maybe the game ended because I was moving the wrong block or in the wrong direction.
Actually, let me reconsider. In the previous attempt, I moved block 2 towards the blue object, and the game ended. But maybe the game ended because I made too many actions, not because of the block's position.
Let me try a different approach: move only block 2 towards the blue object and see if the level completes when they touch.
Block 2 is at (35-36, 32-33). I need to move it up-left to (31-32, 28-29) to touch the blue object. That's 4 moves: (34-35, 31-32), (33-34, 30-31), (32-33, 29-30), (31-32, 28-29).
Let me try this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try a completely different hypothesis.
# Maybe the goal is to move ONE block to touch the blue object.
# Block 2 is closest to the blue object.
# Block 2: (35-36, 32-33), blue object: (23-31, 29-37)
# Moving block 2 up-left 4 times would bring it to (31-32, 28-29), touching the blue object.

# Let me move block 2 towards the blue object
# Current block 2 position: (35-36, 32-33), click (35, 32) to move

sequence = [
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 32},   # Move block 2: (34-35, 31-32)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 31},   # Move block 2: (33-34, 30-31)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 30},   # Move block 2: (32-33, 29-30)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 29},   # Move block 2: (31-32, 28-29) - should touch blue object
]

action(sequence)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
m_blocks = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print(f"Magenta blocks: {len(m_blocks)}")
for m in sorted(m_blocks, key=lambda x: (x['boundary'][0][0], x['boundary'][0][1])):
    print(f"  boundary={m['boundary']}")
print("State:", last_action_result.get('state'))
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
